pages.title: Last mile navigation to Hakka Yu
pages.excerpt: First, travel to Jurong East MRT station and take Exit B. After passing through the ticket gate, turn left and you will see JEM. Enter JEM and continue straight until you see an elevator beside an escalator. Take the elevator up one floor to L3. When you step out, turn around and walk strai
pages.text: First, travel to Jurong East MRT station and take Exit B. After passing through the ticket gate, turn left and you will see JEM. Enter JEM and continue straight until you see an elevator beside an escalator. Take the elevator up one floor to L3. When you step out, turn around and walk straight ahead. You will see Hakka Yu.

posts.title: Does nearest neighbor distance approach farthest neighbor distance in high-dimensional space?
posts.excerpt: I was reading a classical ANN paper that discusses the cases when the distance difference between the nearest neighbor and the farthest neighbor becomes negligible in high-dimensional space. In Section 3.5.3, it provides an interesting manually constructed example, but without detailed proof steps.
posts.text: I was reading a classical ANN paper that discusses the cases when the distance difference between the nearest neighbor and the farthest neighbor becomes negligible in high-dimensional space. In Section 3.5.3, it provides an interesting manually constructed example, but without detailed proof steps. I was writing this blog to complement the process. Problem: Let X‚Éóm=(X1,X2,‚čĮ ,Xm)\vec X_m=(X_1,X_2,\cdots,X_m)Xm‚Äč=(X1‚Äč,X2‚Äč,‚čĮ,Xm‚Äč) be a random mmm-dimensional vector, such that X1=U1X_1=U_1X1‚Äč=U1‚Äč and Xi=Ui+Xi‚ąí1/2X_i=U_i+X_{i-1}/2Xi‚Äč=Ui‚Äč+Xi‚ąí1‚Äč/2, where Ui‚ąľUniform(0,i)U_i\sim \text{Uniform}(0,\sqrt{i})Ui‚Äč‚ąľUniform(0,i‚Äč) is a continuous uniform random variable. Now, we sample two random vectors P‚Éóm,Q‚Éóm\vec P_m,\vec Q_mPm‚Äč,Q‚Äčm‚Äč independently from the distribution of X‚Éóm\vec X_mXm‚Äč. What is the expected L2L_2L2‚Äč distance and variance of distance between P‚Éóm\vec P_mPm‚Äč and Q‚Éóm\vec Q_mQ‚Äčm‚Äč? The conclusion in the paper is 19m2+13m+o(m)\frac{1}{9}m^2+\frac{1}{3}m+o(m)91‚Äčm2+31‚Äčm+o(m), but my results show the more accurate form should be 19m2+127m+o(m)\frac{1}{9}m^2+\frac{1}{27}m+o(m)91‚Äčm2+271‚Äčm+o(m). Solution: The expected distance between P‚Éóm\vec P_mPm‚Äč and Q‚Éóm\vec Q_mQ‚Äčm‚Äč could be computed as follows: E[‚ą£‚ą£P‚Éóm‚ąíQ‚Éóm‚ą£‚ą£22]=E[‚ąĎi=1m(Pi‚ąíQi)2]=‚ąĎi=1mE[(Pi‚ąíQi)2]E\left[||\vec P_m-\vec Q_m||_2^2\right]=E\left[\sum_{i=1}^m (P_i-Q_i)^2\right]=\sum_{i=1}^m E\left[(P_i-Q_i)^2\right] E[‚ą£‚ą£Pm‚Äč‚ąíQ‚Äčm‚Äč‚ą£‚ą£22‚Äč]=E[i=1‚ąĎm‚Äč(Pi‚Äč‚ąíQi‚Äč)2]=i=1‚ąĎm‚ÄčE[(Pi‚Äč‚ąíQi‚Äč)2] Since Var(Pi‚ąíQi)=E[(Pi‚ąíQi)2]‚ąí(E[Pi‚ąíQi])2Var(P_i-Q_i)=E[(P_i-Q_i)^2]-(E[P_i-Q_i])^2Var(Pi‚Äč‚ąíQi‚Äč)=E[(Pi‚Äč‚ąíQi‚Äč)2]‚ąí(E[Pi‚Äč‚ąíQi‚Äč])2 and E[Pi‚ąíQi]=0E[P_i-Q_i]=0E[Pi‚Äč‚ąíQi‚Äč]=0, the above formulae transforms to solving: Var(Pi‚ąíQi)=Var(Pi)+Var(Qi)‚ąí2Cov(Pi,Qi)=2Var(Xi)Var(P_i-Q_i)=Var(P_i)+Var(Q_i)-2Cov(P_i,Q_i)=2Var(X_i) Var(Pi‚Äč‚ąíQi‚Äč)=Var(Pi‚Äč)+Var(Qi‚Äč)‚ąí2Cov(Pi‚Äč,Qi‚Äč)=2Var(Xi‚Äč) Note that Cov(Pi,Qi)=0Cov(P_i,Q_i)=0Cov(Pi‚Äč,Qi‚Äč)=0 by independence. Then, our next step is to compute Var(Xi)Var(X_i)Var(Xi‚Äč). It is not hard to see Xi‚ąí1X_{i-1}Xi‚ąí1‚Äč and UiU_iUi‚Äč are independent. Therefore, Var(Xi)=Var(Ui)+Var(Xi‚ąí1)/4Var(X_i)=Var(U_i)+Var(X_{i-1})/4Var(Xi‚Äč)=Var(Ui‚Äč)+Var(Xi‚ąí1‚Äč)/4. Since Var(Ui)=i/12Var(U_i)=i/12Var(Ui‚Äč)=i/12, we have Var(Xi)=i/12+Var(Xi‚ąí1)/4Var(X_i)=i/12+Var(X_{i-1})/4Var(Xi‚Äč)=i/12+Var(Xi‚ąí1‚Äč)/4. By solving the above recurrence relation, we have: Var(Xi)=127(1/4)i+19i‚ąí127Var(X_i)=\frac{1}{27}(1/4)^i+\frac{1}{9}i-\frac{1}{27} Var(Xi‚Äč)=271‚Äč(1/4)i+91‚Äči‚ąí271‚Äč How to solve the recurrence (I do not know how, following is GPT‚Äôs answer but I verified): We first solve the homogeneous part of the recurrence, which is Var(Xi)=Var(Xi‚ąí1)/4Var(X_i)=Var(X_{i-1})/4Var(Xi‚Äč)=Var(Xi‚ąí1‚Äč)/4. The characteristic equation for this homogeneous part is r=1/4r=1/4r=1/4, so the general solution to the homogeneous part is C(1/4)iC(1/4)^iC(1/4)i for some constant CCC. Next, we find a particular solution to the non-homogeneous recurrence. We can try a particular solution of the form Var(Xi)=A‚ąói+BVar(X_i)=A*i+BVar(Xi‚Äč)=A‚ąói+B for some constants AAA and BBB. Substituting this into the original recurrence gives us: A‚ąói+B=112i+14(A‚ąó(i‚ąí1)+B)‚áíA‚ąói+B=(112+14A)i+14(B‚ąíA)\begin{align} &\quad A*i+B = \frac{1}{12}i + \frac{1}{4}(A*(i-1)+B) \\&\Rightarrow A*i+B=\left(\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{4}A\right)i+\frac{1}{4}(B-A) \end{align} ‚ÄčA‚ąói+B=121‚Äči+41‚Äč(A‚ąó(i‚ąí1)+B)‚áíA‚ąói+B=(121‚Äč+41‚ÄčA)i+41‚Äč(B‚ąíA)‚Äč‚Äč We need to match the coefficients of iii and the constant terms on both sides of the equation. This gives us the following system of equations: {A=112+14AB=14(B‚ąíA)\begin{cases} A=\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{4}A \\ B=\frac{1}{4}(B-A) \end{cases} {A=121‚Äč+41‚ÄčAB=41‚Äč(B‚ąíA)‚Äč Then A=19A=\frac{1}{9}A=91‚Äč and B=‚ąí127B=-\frac{1}{27}B=‚ąí271‚Äč. So the non-homogeneous solution is Var(Xi)=19i‚ąí127Var(X_i)=\frac{1}{9}i-\frac{1}{27}Var(Xi‚Äč)=91‚Äči‚ąí271‚Äč. Putting everything together, the general solution to the original recurrence is Var(Xi)=C(1/4)i+19i‚ąí127Var(X_i)=C(1/4)^i+\frac{1}{9}i-\frac{1}{27}Var(Xi‚Äč)=C(1/4)i+91‚Äči‚ąí271‚Äč. Since Var(X1)=1/12Var(X_1)=1/12Var(X1‚Äč)=1/12, we can solve for CCC to get C=127C=\frac{1}{27}C=271‚Äč. Therefore, the final solution to the recurrence is: Var(Xi)=127(1/4)i+19i‚ąí127Var(X_i)=\frac{1}{27}(1/4)^i+\frac{1}{9}i-\frac{1}{27} Var(Xi‚Äč)=271‚Äč(1/4)i+91‚Äči‚ąí271‚Äč Now back to our goal, we need to summarize 2Var(Xi)2Var(X_i)2Var(Xi‚Äč) for i=1,2,‚čĮ ,mi=1,2,\cdots,mi=1,2,‚čĮ,m to get the expected distance between P‚Éóm\vec P_mPm‚Äč and Q‚Éóm\vec Q_mQ‚Äčm‚Äč: E[‚ą•P‚Éóm‚ąíQ‚Éóm‚ą•22]=‚ąĎi=1m2Var(Xi)=281(1‚ąí14m)+19m(m+1)‚ąí227m=19m2+127m+o(m)\begin{align} E\left[\|\vec P_m-\vec Q_m\|_2^2\right]&=\sum_{i=1}^m 2Var(X_i)=\frac{2}{81}\left(1-\frac{1}{4^m}\right)+\frac{1}{9}m(m+1)-\frac{2}{27}m\\&=\frac{1}{9}m^2+\frac{1}{27}m+o(m) \end{align} E[‚ą•Pm‚Äč‚ąíQ‚Äčm‚Äč‚ą•22‚Äč]‚Äč=i=1‚ąĎm‚Äč2Var(Xi‚Äč)=812‚Äč(1‚ąí4m1‚Äč)+91‚Äčm(m+1)‚ąí272‚Äčm=91‚Äčm2+271‚Äčm+o(m)‚Äč‚Äč I am not writing the variance of distance here since it is much more complicated. Anyway, the difference of our results does not affect the main conclusion of the paper.

posts.title: A reflection on ICPC 2024
posts.excerpt: Preface ICPC 2024 regional has ended, and our team ranked #20 and #38 in Jakarta and Hanoi sites, respectively. As a final year for participating in ICPC as a contestant, I felt quite disappointed and pitiful for the results. Nevertheless, I enjoy the process for training and learning with all NTU c
posts.text: Preface ICPC 2024 regional has ended, and our team ranked #20 and #38 in Jakarta and Hanoi sites, respectively. As a final year for participating in ICPC as a contestant, I felt quite disappointed and pitiful for the results. Nevertheless, I enjoy the process for training and learning with all NTU contestants. I also want to express my sincere gratitude to my teammates, Zong Yu and Min-Jen. Pre-contest preparations In general, I am satisfied with our pre-contest preparations. We had 3 months of training, and each Saturday we virtually participated in a 5-hour contest. I have to admit that the individual capability of our team was not good compared with other NTU teams: our Codeforces rating could not reach 2000 (which is a ‚Äúcandidate master‚ÄĚ), while for other teams, they had at least a person reaching ‚Äúmaster‚ÄĚ rating. This means other teams could clearly have at least one person to ‚Äúcarry‚ÄĚ the team to secure the ‚Äúsolvable‚ÄĚ problems, and their focus was just to improve cooperation and the speed and accuracy for submissions. However, we were struggling to upsolve the problems that other teams could do, and for the training contests, other teams often surpassed us for 1 or 2 problems. The reason is that we were often stuck at some problem. Under such cases, none of us could take ourselves out to find the killing point and most likely we wasted all our time on this problem. As for me, although my coding was okay for the individual contests, I quickly found that I was not suitable for coding in the team working. My coding style was quite different from my teammates, which means whenever I got lost in my codes, others could not debug for me and they had to rewrite the codes. Also, Codeforces problems usually required only a main idea while ICPC problems usually had much more corner cases. When I used Codeforces experience to code in ICPC, even if I was confident before coding, I still often found that I left some cases during coding, and then I got stressed and messed. This also results in my later inconfidence about my ideas in the contest. Luckily, Zong Yu had a much more precise coding style, and he could usually get a clear mind during coding. For example, sometimes I came up with an idea and I thought the coding would be complicated, but he could implement it in a precise way. As a result, we decided to let him implemement even when the idea was proposed by me, except when the problem is very easy. Although this required some extra time for conveying my ideas to him, we could further ensure the correctness of the ideas during the process. Freed from the coding work, I can calm down to analyze the in-contest status and manage the time and targeted problems for my teammates. I also practiced many number theory problems and combinatorial problems, since this required less coding skills and I was the most familiar person with the related knowledge. These types of problems occurred a lot in our UCUP training, however, were not occurring in the regional contests. Min-Jen was good at coding data structures and had strong observations, but sometimes may be stuck on some wrong idea alone. As Zong Yu needed to code and may not have the time to pull him out, I became the person to discuss with him when I noticed that he was in trouble. In summary, Zong Yu and Min-Jen were coders, while I needed to supervise them especially when they were stuck on some ideas and problems. When such cases occurred, I needed to discuss with them, remind them or debug with them. Generally, this cooperation manner is good. I still remembered that for our first training contest, we used three computers and all of us were focusing on our own problem but failed to solve it. And we only solved three problems in that contest. However, as we cooperated better, we could solve 6 to 7 problems in a contest with a single computer. From this time to look back, I think we should reduce the frequency for UCUP training since the end of October. At that time our cooperation has been quite good, but our individual capabilities were still limited, and we should spend more efforts on practicing special topics and individual training. This is even more important to me, as I spent less time in problem solving during the contest compared with my teammates and improved less in team training. Unfortunately, we did not realize this and even put efforts on more training contests. In-contest performance The problem style in Jakarta was quite different from what we were training on. It only had problem C as a very easy problem, and all other problems were kind of tricky. At the very beginning of the contest (first 1 or 2 mins), I opened problem A and found it was easy, so I described the problem and rough idea to Zong Yu and let him code, but then he found that I left an important statement of the problem. Though, he thought he could handle it. Not much later I opened problem C and came up with the solution, but I decided to let Zong Yu code first, until I found that Zong Yu became very stressed and got stuck. So I told Zong Yu to hand over to me to solve C first. I was not confident about my idea so I asked Min-Jen for verification of my codes, and then we submitted it and got accepted. Zong Yu also found out the bug of his idea of problem A and got accepted right after my submission. This was the time that the contest had run for 50 mins, and it was unusual to solve the first problem for that late. Later I spent most of my time on problem B, while Min-Jen and Zong Yu spent their time on problems I and M, and we had discussions on each other‚Äôs problem during the process. Although we slowly caught up with others by solving these 3 problems, I thought I should finish problem B more quickly, as it was not that difficult. Again, it was caused by my limited capabilities. The rest of the contest was about Min-Jen stuck at problem G and Zong Yu stuck at H. The decision that Zong Yu coded H first was because Zong Yu and I already had an idea on H, and when we opened H there were more teams passing H instead of G. When I told Zong Yu my idea on H, I also reminded him to be careful about the time complexity, and the implementation would be very difficult. But Zong Yu was confident: he had practiced string problems before the contest and he thought he already knew how to handle it. Although I was still kind of suspecious, I trusted him and let him code (in training, sometimes I spent a lot of time verifying his codes and ideas and found he was correct; so when he was confident I just trusted him). I also put some time on G to think with Min-Jen, but for most time I was helping with Zong Yu because he was debugging painfully (in fact, I did not offer effective help). I should have realized that Zong Yu was upset debugging his codes, and he should calm down and rethink about his ideas (and our ideas were proved wrong after the contest). However, I did not pull him out and instead I came to him, so we two both got stuck on H. I also noticed that Min-Jen got stuck on G, so I reminded him to have a look at F instead, which is a combinatorial game and he might have some good motivations. Min-Jen read F but had no ideas, so went back to G. For the last 30 minutes, we finally came together for H, but it was too late. As a coordinator, I thought I should not get too involved in debugging with Zong Yu. Instead, I should watch their time and pull them out when necessary. In fact, none of us noticed that we had already spent too much time on our problems. And after Zong Yu calmed down after the contest, he figured out the solution of problem G quickly. As a matter of fact, no one should spend too much time on a single problem. So at that time, it would be better to stop for a while for discussion, and exchange our problems to each other. After Jakarta, Zong Yu realized that he may get stuck on some problems and be upset, so we did extra training on past year‚Äôs Vietnam regionals. I found that Vietnam site had several easy problems and the rest were very hard (means few medium-level problems). Therefore, it would be crucial to solve the easy ones quickly. Moreover, we should be careful about the constant factor in the time complexity, as Vietnam problems were tricky about this. In the contest of Hanoi, we performed really well in the first hour: we solved 5 problems and ranked #5. My teammates performed really well, but I thought I still made some mistakes. For problem C, I made a stupid mistake for the combinatorial formulae, resulting in one unaccepted submission. For problem F which was a case study problem, I realized I did not exhaust all possible cases during coding so I wasted some time. Luckily I calmed down and discussed with Min-Jen, and cleared my mind before the final submission and got accepted. Anyway, if my capability was stronger, I should pass these two problems more accurately and quickly without occupying my teammate‚Äôs time. I also verified the ideas of problem L proposed by Min-Jen, but overall my teammates did the most parts for the rest 3 problems and they did very well. However, the situation turned bad when Min-Jen and Zong Yu were stuck on problem M. At that time I was thinking about problem E, which was a case-study game. I thought for a while and realized that it was difficult, and no teams had passed the problem so I gave up. And then I found that my teammates had been debugging on problem M for a while, so I asked Zong Yu to think about problem K instead. I was skeptical about whether we went into a wrong direction on M, since many teams passed the problem easily, but we debugged for a long time. So I discussed with my teammates to know about their ideas, which was a randomized algorithm, but their ideas sounded so correct and I proved it could pass a test case with high probability (until the last hour, we realized that the probability shrunk when the number of test cases increased). Then I still let Zong Yu help Min-Jen to debug, and I thought about problem K instead. Almost an hour later we finally solved problem K, and then I told Zong Yu to write a stress test for problem M (actually, this should be done earlier). I already knew that we were spending too much time on M, but I had no ideas on other problems. After the stress test was finished, I thought I should not put two people on M, which had become a time-eating problem. So Zong Yu and I started to think about problem B but we both had no idea. Later, I exchange Min-Jen and Zong Yu, to let Zong Yu debug on M and Min-Jen think about B. Min-Jen figured out the idea of B quickly, which was a sum-of-subset dynamic programming, but it was just an hour left. We decided to take the computer in turns for problems B and M, and only used the computer when our minds were clear. With such strategy we finished the program of problem B and finalized M, but then we found our ideas for M were wrong, and there were still some cases to handle for problem B but we were not familiar with such kinds of DP so we put extra time. In the last 5 minutes, we finally figured out the cases and solutions for B, but we had no time to finish them. In summary, I think our strategy is okay, and it is a bad luck that we did not observe a simple fact on M and ask Min-Jen to do B earlier. If I asked Min-Jen to read B first instead of asking Zong Yu, things may get changed, but this was not foreseeable. The fundamental thing is that we did not have enough individual capabilities, especially for me, and I did not contribute to many ideas in the Hanoi site. Post-contest thoughts Up to now, I still sometimes dreamed back to the contest when I fell asleep. Indeed, I need to spend most of my efforts on research and could not exert myself in ICPC contests. But I think even with limited time for preparation, I could do better than what I did. For example, I should do more individual trainings to improve my capabilities. Then I may contribute more ideas during the contests apart from the management. Although I upsolved many problems after each team training, that was quite different from what you felt during contests. Upsolving problems are like research, and you can think about it in a slower pace, so that your mind gets much more clear. However, during the contests, I need to spare some efforts to my teammates, and would get stressed under the time limit. So thinking back to preparation, I should do specific topics one by one instead of randomly upsolving problems, and take more individual contests. But overall, although the results are disappointing, I do not regret participating this year‚Äôs ICPC.

posts.title: The Pisano period of C-finite sequences
posts.excerpt: Problem definition The Pisano period of a prime ppp, is defined as the minimum integer ŌÄ(p)\pi(p)ŌÄ(p) satisfying f0‚Č°fŌÄ(p),f1‚Č°fŌÄ(p)+1(mod‚Ā°p)f_0\equiv f_{\pi(p)},f_1\equiv f_{\pi(p)+1}(\operatorname{mod} p)f0‚Äč‚Č°fŌÄ(p)‚Äč,f1‚Äč‚Č°fŌÄ(p)+1‚Äč(modp), where fff is the Fibonacci sequence. It is well known that for p=
posts.text:
Problem definition The Pisano period of a prime ppp, is defined as the minimum integer ŌÄ(p)\pi(p)ŌÄ(p) satisfying f0‚Č°fŌÄ(p),f1‚Č°fŌÄ(p)+1(mod‚Ā°p)f_0\equiv f_{\pi(p)},f_1\equiv f_{\pi(p)+1}(\operatorname{mod} p)f0‚Äč‚Č°fŌÄ(p)‚Äč,f1‚Äč‚Č°fŌÄ(p)+1‚Äč(modp), where fff is the Fibonacci sequence. It is well known that for p=Őł2,5p
ot=2,5pÓÄ†=2,5, ŌÄ(p)\pi(p)ŌÄ(p) is either a divisor of p‚ąí1p-1p‚ąí1 or 2p+22p+22p+2. In this article, we extend this conclusion to any C-finite sequence of the formulae fn‚Č°afn‚ąí1+fn‚ąí2(mod‚Ā°p)f_n\equiv af_{n-1}+f_{n-2}(\operatorname{mod} p)fn‚Äč‚Č°afn‚ąí1‚Äč+fn‚ąí2‚Äč(modp), where a‚ąąZpa\in\mathbb{Z}_pa‚ąąZp‚Äč is a constant. Closed form expression Assume fnf_nfn‚Äč has a general form rnr^nrn. Then we rewrite the recursive formulae as rn‚Č°arn‚ąí1+rn‚ąí2r^n\equiv ar^{n-1}+r^{n-2}rn‚Č°arn‚ąí1+rn‚ąí2, which yields a characteristic equation r2‚ąíar‚ąí1‚Č°0r^2-ar-1\equiv 0r2‚ąíar‚ąí1‚Č°0. Let őĽ1,őĽ2\lambda_1,\lambda_2őĽ1‚Äč,őĽ2‚Äč be the characteristic roots. Assume f0=0,f1=1f_0=0,f_1=1f0‚Äč=0,f1‚Äč=1, W.L.O.G., We claim that: fn‚Č°őĽ1n‚ąíőĽ2nőĽ1‚ąíőĽ2f_n\equiv rac{\lambda_1^n-\lambda_2^n}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2} fn‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2‚ÄčőĽ1n‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2n‚Äč‚Äč Proof: since fn‚ąí1‚Č°őĽ1n‚ąí1‚ąíőĽ2n‚ąí1őĽ1‚ąíőĽ2f_{n-1}\equiv rac{\lambda_1^{n-1}-\lambda_2^{n-1}}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2}fn‚ąí1‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2‚ÄčőĽ1n‚ąí1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2n‚ąí1‚Äč‚Äč and fn‚ąí2‚Č°őĽ1n‚ąí2‚ąíőĽ2n‚ąí2őĽ1‚ąíőĽ2f_{n-2}\equiv rac{\lambda_1^{n-2}-\lambda_2^{n-2}}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2}fn‚ąí2‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2‚ÄčőĽ1n‚ąí2‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2n‚ąí2‚Äč‚Äč, afn‚ąí1+fn‚ąí2‚Č°aőĽ1n‚ąí1‚ąíaőĽ2n‚ąí1+őĽ1n‚ąí2‚ąíőĽ2n‚ąí2őĽ1‚ąíőĽ2‚Č°őĽ1n‚ąí2(aőĽ1+1)‚ąíőĽ2n‚ąí2(aőĽ2+1)őĽ1‚ąíőĽ2‚Č°őĽ1n‚ąíőĽ2nőĽ1‚ąíőĽ2‚Č°fn egin{aligned} af_{n-1}+f_{n-2}&\equiv rac{a\lambda_1^{n-1}-a\lambda_2^{n-1}+\lambda_1^{n-2}-\lambda_2^{n-2}}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2}\ &\equiv rac{\lambda_1^{n-2}(a\lambda_1+1)-\lambda_2^{n-2}(a\lambda_2+1)}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2}\ &\equiv rac{\lambda_1^n-\lambda_2^n}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2}\ &\equiv f_n \end{aligned} afn‚ąí1‚Äč+fn‚ąí2‚Äč‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2‚ÄčaőĽ1n‚ąí1‚Äč‚ąíaőĽ2n‚ąí1‚Äč+őĽ1n‚ąí2‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2n‚ąí2‚Äč‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2‚ÄčőĽ1n‚ąí2‚Äč(aőĽ1‚Äč+1)‚ąíőĽ2n‚ąí2‚Äč(aőĽ2‚Äč+1)‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2‚ÄčőĽ1n‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2n‚Äč‚Äč‚Č°fn‚Äč‚Äč Pisano period As aforementioned, the characteristic roots should satisfy r2‚ąíar‚ąí1‚Č°0r^2-ar-1\equiv 0r2‚ąíar‚ąí1‚Č°0 or (2r‚ąía)2‚Č°a2+4(mod‚Ā°p)(2r-a)^2\equiv a^2+4(\operatorname{mod}p)(2r‚ąía)2‚Č°a2+4(modp). If ‚ąÉw‚ąąZp\exists w\in\mathbb{Z}_p‚ąÉw‚ąąZp‚Äč such that w2‚Č°a2+4(mod‚Ā°p)w^2\equiv a^2+4(\operatorname{mod} p)w2‚Č°a2+4(modp), it is straightforward to derive 2őĽ1‚ąía‚Č°w2\lambda_1-a\equiv w2őĽ1‚Äč‚ąía‚Č°w and 2őĽ2‚ąía‚Č°‚ąíw2\lambda_2-a\equiv-w2őĽ2‚Äč‚ąía‚Č°‚ąíw and thus őĽ1,őĽ2‚ąąZp\lambda_1,\lambda_2\in\mathbb{Z}_pőĽ1‚Äč,őĽ2‚Äč‚ąąZp‚Äč. Then by Fermat‚Äôs little theorem, őĽ1p‚ąí1‚Č°őĽ2p‚ąí1‚Č°1\lambda_1^{p-1}\equiv\lambda_2^{p-1}\equiv 1őĽ1p‚ąí1‚Äč‚Č°őĽ2p‚ąí1‚Äč‚Č°1 and: fn+p‚ąí1‚Č°őĽ1n+p‚ąí1‚ąíőĽ2n+p‚ąí1őĽ1‚ąíőĽ2‚Č°őĽ1n‚ąíőĽ2nőĽ1‚ąíőĽ2‚Č°fnf_{n+p-1}\equiv rac{\lambda_1^{n+p-1}-\lambda_2^{n+p-1}}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2}\equiv rac{\lambda_1^n-\lambda_2^n}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2}\equiv f_{n} fn+p‚ąí1‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2‚ÄčőĽ1n+p‚ąí1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2n+p‚ąí1‚Äč‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2‚ÄčőĽ1n‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2n‚Äč‚Äč‚Č°fn‚Äč which implies that the Pisano period ŌÄ(p)‚ą£(p‚ąí1)\pi(p)|(p-1)ŌÄ(p)‚ą£(p‚ąí1). If ‚ąÉŐłw‚ąąZp
ot\exists w\in\mathbb{Z}_pÓÄ†‚ąÉw‚ąąZp‚Äč such that w2‚Č°a2+4(mod‚Ā°p)w^2\equiv a^2+4(\operatorname{mod} p)w2‚Č°a2+4(modp), we claim that ŌÄ(p)‚ą£(2p+2)\pi(p)|(2p+2)ŌÄ(p)‚ą£(2p+2). Proof: since őĽ1,őĽ2\lambda_1,\lambda_2őĽ1‚Äč,őĽ2‚Äč are the roots of the equation r2‚ąíar‚ąí1‚Č°0r^2-ar-1\equiv 0r2‚ąíar‚ąí1‚Č°0, őĽ1+őĽ2‚Č°a\lambda_1+\lambda_2\equiv aőĽ1‚Äč+őĽ2‚Äč‚Č°a and őĽ1őĽ2‚Č°‚ąí1\lambda_1\lambda_2\equiv -1őĽ1‚ÄčőĽ2‚Äč‚Č°‚ąí1. Then: (w+a)p2‚ąíp+őĽ2p‚Č°ap(w+a)^p2^{-p}+\lambda_2^p\equiv a^p (w+a)p2‚ąíp+őĽ2p‚Äč‚Č°ap Note that őĽ1,őĽ2,w‚ąąŐłZp\lambda_1,\lambda_2,w
ot\in\mathbb{Z}_pőĽ1‚Äč,őĽ2‚Äč,wÓÄ†‚ąąZp‚Äč and 2,a‚ąąZp2,a\in\mathbb{Z}_p2,a‚ąąZp‚Äč. Therefore, (2‚ąí1)p‚ąí1‚Č°ap‚ąí1‚Č°1\left(2^{-1}ight)^{p-1}\equiv a^{p-1}\equiv 1(2‚ąí1)p‚ąí1‚Č°ap‚ąí1‚Č°1 by Fermeat‚Äôs little theorem: (w+a)p+2őĽ2p‚Č°2ap‚áíwp+2őĽ2p‚Č°ap‚Č°a‚áíőĽ2p‚Č°2‚ąí1(a‚ąíwp) egin{aligned} &\quad (w+a)^p+2\lambda_2^p\equiv 2a^p\ &\Rightarrow w^p+2\lambda_2^p\equiv a^p\equiv a\ &\Rightarrow \lambda_2^p\equiv 2^{-1}(a-w^p) \end{aligned} ‚Äč(w+a)p+2őĽ2p‚Äč‚Č°2ap‚áíwp+2őĽ2p‚Äč‚Č°ap‚Č°a‚áíőĽ2p‚Äč‚Č°2‚ąí1(a‚ąíwp)‚Äč By quadratic reciprocity, wp‚ąí1‚Č°(a2+4)p‚ąí12‚Č°‚ąí1w^{p-1}\equiv (a^2+4)^{ rac{p-1}{2}}\equiv -1wp‚ąí1‚Č°(a2+4)2p‚ąí1‚Äč‚Č°‚ąí1, then: őĽ2p‚Č°2‚ąí1(a+w)‚Č°őĽ1\lambda_2^p\equiv2^{-1}(a+w)\equiv \lambda_1 őĽ2p‚Äč‚Č°2‚ąí1(a+w)‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč Then őĽ2p+1‚Č°őĽ1őĽ2‚Č°‚ąí1\lambda_2^{p+1}\equiv \lambda_1\lambda_2\equiv -1őĽ2p+1‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚ÄčőĽ2‚Äč‚Č°‚ąí1 and őĽ22p+2‚Č°1\lambda_2^{2p+2}\equiv 1őĽ22p+2‚Äč‚Č°1. Similarly, we can prove őĽ12p+2‚Č°1\lambda_1^{2p+2}\equiv 1őĽ12p+2‚Äč‚Č°1 by assuming w=2őĽ2‚ąíaw=2\lambda_2-aw=2őĽ2‚Äč‚ąía. Therefore: fn+2p+2‚Č°őĽ1n+2p+2‚ąíőĽ2n+2p+2őĽ1‚ąíőĽ2‚Č°őĽ1n‚ąíőĽ2nőĽ1‚ąíőĽ2‚Č°fnf_{n+2p+2}\equiv rac{\lambda_1^{n+2p+2}-\lambda_2^{n+2p+2}}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2}\equiv rac{\lambda_1^n-\lambda_2^n}{\lambda_1-\lambda_2}\equiv f_n fn+2p+2‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2‚ÄčőĽ1n+2p+2‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2n+2p+2‚Äč‚Äč‚Č°őĽ1‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2‚ÄčőĽ1n‚Äč‚ąíőĽ2n‚Äč‚Äč‚Č°fn‚Äč

posts.title: Graph coloring with densest subgraph
posts.excerpt: Problem definition An undirected graph GGG is kkk-coloring if all vertices could be colored with at most kkk different colors, such that all adjacent vertices have different colors. For example, a kkk-clique is kkk-coloring, and a tree is 2-coloring. The densest subgraph of a graph GGG, is an induce
posts.text: Problem definition An undirected graph GGG is kkk-coloring if all vertices could be colored with at most kkk different colors, such that all adjacent vertices have different colors. For example, a kkk-clique is kkk-coloring, and a tree is 2-coloring. The densest subgraph of a graph GGG, is an induced subgraph of a vertex subset SSS of GGG with nSn_SnS‚Äč vertices and mSm_SmS‚Äč edges, such that its density (i.e., ŌĀmax=2mSnS\rho_{max}=\frac{2m_S}{n_S}ŌĀmax‚Äč=nS‚Äč2mS‚Äč‚Äč) is highest among all induced subgraphs. In this article, we aim to prove that for a graph GGG whose densest subgraph has a density ŌĀmax\rho_{max}ŌĀmax‚Äč, it is ‚ĆąŌĀmax+1‚ĆČ\lceil\rho_{max}+1\rceil‚ĆąŌĀmax‚Äč+1‚ĆČ-coloring. Proof Let G=(V,E)G=(V,E)G=(V,E) and n=‚ą£V‚ą£,m=‚ą£E‚ą£n=|V|,m=|E|n=‚ą£V‚ą£,m=‚ą£E‚ą£. We prove it by induction. When n=1n=1n=1, ŌĀmax=0\rho_{max}=0ŌĀmax‚Äč=0 and the graph is 1-coloring. Assume we have proved the case for n=in=in=i. For any graph GGG such that n=i+1n=i+1n=i+1, we denote SSS as the vertex set of its densest subgraph and assume its density is ŌĀmax\rho_{max}ŌĀmax‚Äč. Then we remove the smallest degree vertex uuu of GGG and derives a graph with iii vertices, which is proved to be ‚ĆąŌĀmax‚Ä≤+1‚ĆČ\lceil\rho_{max}'+1\rceil‚ĆąŌĀmax‚Ä≤‚Äč+1‚ĆČ-coloring and ŌĀmax‚Ä≤\rho_{max}'ŌĀmax‚Ä≤‚Äč is the largest density of the new graph. Obviously, the degree of uuu is no larger than ŌĀmax\rho_{max}ŌĀmax‚Äč. Otherwise, the total degree of all vertices in GGG is larger than ŌĀmaxn\rho_{max}nŌĀmax‚Äčn, which yields a density larger than ŌĀmax\rho_{max}ŌĀmax‚Äč. Therefore, uuu can be colored with at most ‚ĆąŌĀmax+1‚ĆČ\lceil\rho_{max}+1\rceil‚ĆąŌĀmax‚Äč+1‚ĆČ colors (if only considering its neighbours). We claim that ŌĀmax‚Ä≤‚Č§ŌĀmax\rho_{max}'\leq\rho_{max}ŌĀmax‚Ä≤‚Äč‚Č§ŌĀmax‚Äč. Then GGG is ‚ĆąŌĀmax+1‚ĆČ\lceil\rho_{max}+1\rceil‚ĆąŌĀmax‚Äč+1‚ĆČ-coloring (note that if a graph is aaa-coloring and a‚Č§ba\leq ba‚Č§b, then it is also bbb-coloring). We prove it by contradiction. If ŌĀmax‚Ä≤>ŌĀmax\rho_{max}'>\rho_{max}ŌĀmax‚Ä≤‚Äč>ŌĀmax‚Äč, removing uuu results in some induced subgraph with a larger density. However, by definition, the densest subgraph of GGG is already the induced subgraph with the highest density. Therefore, it is impossible to find a denser subgraph by removing a vertex in GGG. By induction, the proof is done. Finding a coloring scheme We recursively remove the smallest degree vertex in GGG. Specifically, we maintain a min-heap storing the degrees of all vertices. Initially, we insert all vertices into the heap. For each round, we extract the minimum degree vertex from the heap and remove the vertex. During the remove process, we need to update the degree of its neighbors (for a simple graph, the new degree of its neighbors dnewd_{new}dnew‚Äč should be dold‚ąí1d_{old}-1dold‚Äč‚ąí1) and adjust the heap. With a binary heap, since extracting each vertex and updating the degree of each vertex is O(log‚Ā°n)O(\log n)O(logn), and there are mmm updates in total, the time complexity would be O(mlog‚Ā°n)O(m\log n)O(mlogn). With a Fibonacci heap, where decrease-key function could be done in O(1)O(1)O(1), the time complexity would reduce to O(m+nlog‚Ā°n)O(m+n\log n)O(m+nlogn). We record the sequence a1,a2,‚čĮ ,ana_1,a_2,\cdots,a_na1‚Äč,a2‚Äč,‚čĮ,an‚Äč of removing vertices, i.e., the algorithm removes a1,a2,‚čĮ ,ana_1,a_2,\cdots,a_na1‚Äč,a2‚Äč,‚čĮ,an‚Äč sequentially. To construct the coloring scheme, we reversely consider each vertex: let G‚Ä≤G'G‚Ä≤ be an empty graph. We firstly add ana_nan‚Äč to G‚Ä≤G'G‚Ä≤ and color it with 111. Then we add an‚ąí1a_{n-1}an‚ąí1‚Äč to G‚Ä≤G'G‚Ä≤ and its corresponding edges in GGG connecting ana_nan‚Äč (if exist). If an,an‚ąí1a_n,a_{n-1}an‚Äč,an‚ąí1‚Äč are connected, we color an‚ąí1a_{n-1}an‚ąí1‚Äč with 222. Otherwise, it is colored with 111. Similarly, we add an‚ąí2a_{n-2}an‚ąí2‚Äč to G‚Ä≤G'G‚Ä≤ and its edges to an‚ąí1,ana_{n-1},a_nan‚ąí1‚Äč,an‚Äč if exist, and color an‚ąí2a_{n-2}an‚ąí2‚Äč with the minimum possible color. Repeat this process until a1a_1a1‚Äč is inserted into GGG and colored. The time complexity of this process is bounded by O(m)O(m)O(m). Therefore, the total time complexity of the algorithm is bounded by O(m+nlog‚Ā°n)O(m+n\log n)O(m+nlogn), and it is straightforward to prove its correctness using the similar technique in the induction proof.

posts.title: Maintaining the diameter of a growing tree in O(log n) time
posts.excerpt: A tree is a connected undirected graph with nnn nodes and n‚ąí1n-1n‚ąí1 edges. The diameter of a tree is the largest distance between two nodes in the tree. We define a growing tree as follows: at each moment, a new node uuu is inserted into the tree, and uuu is connected to some node vvv in the origin
posts.text: A tree is a connected undirected graph with nnn nodes and n‚ąí1n-1n‚ąí1 edges. The diameter of a tree is the largest distance between two nodes in the tree. We define a growing tree as follows: at each moment, a new node uuu is inserted into the tree, and uuu is connected to some node vvv in the original tree. What is the diameter of the new tree? Let LLL be the path such that the length of LLL is the diameter of the original tree. A key observation is that if w1,w2w_1,w_2w1‚Äč,w2‚Äč are the endpoints of LLL, then for the new tree, the endpoints of the new path for the new diameter must be (u,w1),(u,w2)(u,w_1),(u,w_2)(u,w1‚Äč),(u,w2‚Äč) or (w1,w2)(w_1,w_2)(w1‚Äč,w2‚Äč). The proof is rather simple. The distance between w1,w2w_1,w_2w1‚Äč,w2‚Äč is actually the length of LLL. By the definition of the tree diameter, it is also the largest distance between two nodes in the original tree. Since the distances between two nodes do not change in the original and new tree, the only candidate nodes to be the new endpoints could only be u,w1,w2u,w_1,w_2u,w1‚Äč,w2‚Äč (for other nodes, since they have the same distances which are not larger than ‚ą£L‚ą£|L|‚ą£L‚ą£, we could just ignore them). Therefore, to derive the new diamter, we only need to maintain w1,w2w_1,w_2w1‚Äč,w2‚Äč for each moment, and compute the distances between (u,w1),(u,w2)(u,w_1),(u,w_2)(u,w1‚Äč),(u,w2‚Äč) and (w1,w2)(w_1,w_2)(w1‚Äč,w2‚Äč) after each moment, where the largest distance is the new diameter. The distance between (u,w1)(u,w_1)(u,w1‚Äč) could be computed by d(u)+d(w1)‚ąí2d(LCA(u,w1))d(u)+d(w_1)-2d(LCA(u,w_1))d(u)+d(w1‚Äč)‚ąí2d(LCA(u,w1‚Äč)), where ddd is the depth of the node and LCALCALCA is the lowest common ancestor of two nodes, and other distances could be computed similarly. The depth of the new node uuu could be maintained in O(1)O(1)O(1) time for each moment. Maintaining and computing LCALCALCA could be done in O(log‚Ā°n)O(\log n)O(logn) time for each moment. As a result, we could maintain the diameter of a growing tree in O(log‚Ā°n)O(\log n)O(logn) time.

posts.title: The XOR sum of 4x, 4x + 1, 4x + 2 and 4x + 3 is 0 for any non-negative integer x
posts.excerpt: In this article, we would use ‚äē\oplus‚äē to represent the bitwise XOR operation between two integers. We observe that: for any x‚ąąNx\in\mathbb Nx‚ąąN, (4x)‚äē(4x+1)‚äē(4x+2)‚äē(4x+3)=0(4x)\oplus(4x+1)\oplus(4x+2)\oplus(4x+3)=0(4x)‚äē(4x+1)‚äē(4x+2)‚äē(4x+3)=0, and therefore, ‚®Āi=04y‚ąí1i=0\bigoplus_{i=0}^{4y-1}i=0‚®Āi=04
posts.text: In this article, we would use ‚äē\oplus‚äē to represent the bitwise XOR operation between two integers. We observe that: for any x‚ąąNx\in\mathbb Nx‚ąąN, (4x)‚äē(4x+1)‚äē(4x+2)‚äē(4x+3)=0(4x)\oplus(4x+1)\oplus(4x+2)\oplus(4x+3)=0(4x)‚äē(4x+1)‚äē(4x+2)‚äē(4x+3)=0, and therefore, ‚®Āi=04y‚ąí1i=0\bigoplus_{i=0}^{4y-1}i=0‚®Āi=04y‚ąí1‚Äči=0 for any y‚ąąN+y\in\mathbb N^+y‚ąąN+. We now illustrate the formal proof of the above observation. We firstly write xxx into a binary format, i.e.: x=a0+2a1+22a2+‚čĮ+2nan=(anan‚ąí1‚čĮa0)2,ai‚ąą{0,1}x=a_0+2a_1+2^2a_2+\cdots+2^na_n=(a_na_{n-1}\cdots a_0)_2,a_i\in\lbrace 0,1\rbrace x=a0‚Äč+2a1‚Äč+22a2‚Äč+‚čĮ+2nan‚Äč=(an‚Äčan‚ąí1‚Äč‚čĮa0‚Äč)2‚Äč,ai‚Äč‚ąą{0,1} Let k=4xk=4xk=4x. Then: k=22a0+23a1+‚čĮ+2n+2ank=2^2a_0+2^3a_1+\cdots+2^{n+2}a_n k=22a0‚Äč+23a1‚Äč+‚čĮ+2n+2an‚Äč k+1=1+22a0+23a1+‚čĮ+2n+2ank+1=1+2^2a_0+2^3a_1+\cdots+2^{n+2}a_n k+1=1+22a0‚Äč+23a1‚Äč+‚čĮ+2n+2an‚Äč k+2=2+22a0+23a1+‚čĮ+2n+2ank+2=2+2^2a_0+2^3a_1+\cdots+2^{n+2}a_n k+2=2+22a0‚Äč+23a1‚Äč+‚čĮ+2n+2an‚Äč k+3=1+2+22a0+23a1+‚čĮ+2n+2ank+3=1+2+2^2a_0+2^3a_1+\cdots+2^{n+2}a_n k+3=1+2+22a0‚Äč+23a1‚Äč+‚čĮ+2n+2an‚Äč Then the binary formats of k,k+1,k+2,k+3k,k+1,k+2,k+3k,k+1,k+2,k+3 are: k=(anan‚ąí1‚čĮ00)2k=(a_na_{n-1}\cdots 00)_2 k=(an‚Äčan‚ąí1‚Äč‚čĮ00)2‚Äč k+1=(anan‚ąí1‚čĮ01)2k+1=(a_na_{n-1}\cdots 01)_2 k+1=(an‚Äčan‚ąí1‚Äč‚čĮ01)2‚Äč k+2=(anan‚ąí1‚čĮ10)2k+2=(a_na_{n-1}\cdots 10)_2 k+2=(an‚Äčan‚ąí1‚Äč‚čĮ10)2‚Äč k+3=(anan‚ąí1‚čĮ11)2k+3=(a_na_{n-1}\cdots 11)_2 k+3=(an‚Äčan‚ąí1‚Äč‚čĮ11)2‚Äč Since the (n+1)(n+1)(n+1) most significant bits of all aforementioned numbers are the same, the XOR sum of this part should be 0. For the latter bits, i.e., the 2 least significant bits, it is easy to compute that: (00)‚äē(01)‚äē(10)‚äē(11)=(00)(00)\oplus(01)\oplus(10)\oplus(11)=(00) (00)‚äē(01)‚äē(10)‚äē(11)=(00) Therefore, k‚äē(k+1)‚äē(k+2)‚äē(k+3)=0k\oplus(k+1)\oplus(k+2)\oplus(k+3)=0k‚äē(k+1)‚äē(k+2)‚äē(k+3)=0, and the proof is done.

posts.title: Welcome to my blog!
posts.excerpt: In my very early ages, I have planned to set up a personal blog. However, all my personal blogs were discarded either because I felt tired to maintain it or I felt nobody was going to read my posts. And as you see, this is my another attempt, and I would try my best to maintain this blog for this ti
posts.text: In my very early ages, I have planned to set up a personal blog. However, all my personal blogs were discarded either because I felt tired to maintain it or I felt nobody was going to read my posts. And as you see, this is my another attempt, and I would try my best to maintain this blog for this time. I know some of you may be my friends or you just come from my homepage. Different from my homepage which highlights my research publications, this blogs would record some personal thoughts, and some interesting ideas that may be too trivial to publish a paper. I may publish posts in multiple fields, including maths, computer science and so on (if I learn something new in the future). The thoughts may be quite random, and I would try to keep the posts brief to avoid them from being messed up. Initially, I want to start my blog page with sharing of personal experiences. But, I don‚Äôt know whether everyone would be interested in it. Therefore, the welcome page ends here, and I would fill in more contents via subsequent posts.
